seoTitle: AI in the product: where, guardrails, and POC before build | Luc Rousseau
seoDescription: AI in your product (live or new): where it fits, guardrails, POC before prod, product and technical decisions, not hype.

hero.title: AI in your product
hero.lede:
AI is on the table, whether your product is already live or in launch. « We need AI » on the roadmap, agency proposes a fuzzy feature, team experiments without guardrails. Risk: cost, data, UX, and debt, without clear business value.

I work on where AI belongs, how to prototype and test fast, what criteria for prod, not hype, not a black box. Same thread: product, architecture, and execution on high-leverage topics.

blocks.title: AI feature because the market asks
blocks.lede: Without clear criteria, you ship a chatbot or auto-summary nobody uses, or can't maintain. Data, cost, quality, fallback when the model changes, unanswered until incident.

blocks.title: Product AI strategy, not a parallel lab

items.title: Where AI actually helps the product
items.content: Use-case map: user gain, frequency, error tolerance, non-AI alternative, to say no to decorative ideas.

items.title: Guardrails and prod criteria
items.content: Data, privacy, cost per request, fallback, monitoring, human review if needed, written before build, not after first complaint.

items.title: POC and fast prototypes
items.content: Test hypotheses with POCs or AI flows, before committing the team to months of dev. Unlike the PM guide: angle here is product + technical + risk.

items.title: Fit with architecture
items.content: Where logic lives (API, async, cache), how it integrates with the rest of the SaaS, not a brick glued beside the product.

blocks.title: « We'll add AI » vs product AI framing
blocks.intro: Complements PM/PO need; here the focus is technical decision and product risk.

headers.framedFirst: Product AI framing then targeted build

rows.framedFirst: Hypothesis and metric defined before code.

rows.hypeFirst: Vendor black box or scattered experiments.
rows.framedFirst: POC → prerequisites → build with clear acceptance criteria.
Product-tech trade-offs; not daily dev management.

blocks.title: Technical foundations
blocks.lede: AI in your existing product: where it fits, guardrails, cost, and POC before production build.

items: Handover to the internal dev team

items.title: Do you implement the models yourself?
items.content: Yes, on high-leverage topics: I can build POCs and targeted integrations; long-term run stays with you or your agency, with a spec they can execute.

items.title: We already have a PM: isn't that redundant?
items.content: Often no. A PM covers prioritization and day-to-day rituals. This work covers where AI fits, risks, architecture, and prototypes before prod. The two complement each other if you need both.

items.title: AI changes too fast, why plan?
items.content: Exactly: product criteria and guardrails stay stable even when the model changes, that's what avoids rebuilding on every vendor announcement.

items.title: Reachable outside scheduled days, or in an emergency?
items.content:
The agreed rhythm structures deep work, not whether you can reach me.

Ongoing, I make time to reply on Slack or your preferred channel. If an emergency lands outside scheduled days, I stay reachable and step in under a shared urgency frame we define up front (scope, priority, response time), not a vague 24/7 on-call promise.

blocks.label: Discuss product AI
blocks.teaser: Pressure to add AI without a frame? One call to see where it makes sense, how to prototype, and what deserves prod.
